```diff
+status/logs, use the `cloudflare-api` MCP tool against

```

1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
4	
5	## What this is
6	
7	A Hugo static site for Rod Begbie's personal/coaching site (`coaching.begbie.com`), using a
8	vendored copy of the **portio** theme (Bootstrap 5.3.8). Deployed to Cloudflare Pages via `wrangler`.
9	
10	## Commands
11	
12	```bash
13	hugo server -D        # local dev server with drafts, live reload
14	hugo                   # build to public/ (generated, gitignored)
15	```
16	
17	Hugo version is pinned to **0.163.3** — set via `HUGO_VERSION` in `wrangler.jsonc`, which is what
18	Cloudflare Pages reads to pick the build image. There is no package.json, Makefile, or test suite;
19	content/template correctness is verified by building and checking `public/` or the dev server.
20	
21	## Architecture
22	
23	**Theme is vendored, not a submodule.** `themes/portio` is a full copy of the theme committed
24	directly into this repo (see commit `55bde54`, "Copy in the portio theme, instead of submoduling
25	it"). `.gitmodules` exists but is empty — don't expect `git submodule update` to do anything.
26	Theme edits happen directly under `themes/portio/layouts`; there is no project-level `layouts/`
27	override directory.
28	
29	**Homepage is built from toggleable, data-driven sections.** Each section partial in
30	`themes/portio/layouts/partials/` (e.g. `aboutSection.html`, `heroSection` via `hero.html`,
31	`resumeSection.html`, `serviceSection.html`, `skillSection.html`, `portfolioSection.html`,
32	`testimonialSection.html`, `blogSection.html`) reads its content from a same-named YAML file in
33	`data/` (e.g. `data/aboutSection.yml`). Every data file starts with `enable: true|false`, and the
34	partial wraps itself in `{{ if .enable }}` — flip that flag to show/hide a section, no template
35	changes needed. Currently disabled: `portfolioSection`, `serviceSection`, `skillSection`.
36	
37	**Data is read via `hugo.Data.<name>`, not `.Site.Data.<name>`.** This was changed in commit
38	`d4d2e42` ("Fix Hugo compatibility for modern versions") when upgrading to Hugo 0.157 — `.Site.Data`
39	is deprecated and `resources.ToCSS` was replaced with `css.Sass`. If you add a new data-driven
40	section or touch SCSS compilation, follow these same patterns rather than older portio-theme
41	examples found online (`themes/portio/exampleSite` still uses the old API and is reference-only,
42	not built).
43	
44	**Design tokens live in `themes/portio/assets/scss/_tokens.scss`.** CSS custom properties
45	(`--accent`, `--ink`, `--bg`, etc.) plus Sass variables for fonts/spacing/radii. New components
46	should reference these, not hardcode hex/px values.
47	
48	**Decorative blob backgrounds use `layouts/partials/blob.html`**, called as
49	`{{ partial "blob.html" (dict "id" "unique-gradient-id" "color" "var(--accent)" "stopOpacity" ".08") }}`
50	inside a `.blob-wrap` wrapper div. One shared partial for every section's blob — don't hand-copy
51	the inline SVG again.
52	
53	**Résumé data is split into `recent`/`earlier` lists**, not one flat list sliced by `first N`.
54	`data/resumeSection.yml`'s `recent` entries render as cards; `earlier` entries generate the
55	"Earlier: ..." summary line directly in the template. Add a new job by moving the oldest `recent`
56	entry into `earlier`, not by editing prose by hand.
57	
58	**Content types:**
59	- `content/blog/*.md` — blog posts (front matter: `title`, `date`, `featureImage`,
60	  `featureImageAnchor` (optional, overrides smart-crop anchor — see below), `subtitle`), rendered
61	  through `themes/portio/layouts/blog/{list,single}.html`.
62	- There is no `/contact/` page — removed (was unlinked from nav/hero/footer, its form posted to an
63	  unconfigured Formspree placeholder). Site contact is the homepage's `#contact` footer CTA
64	  (mailto link) or `data/aboutSection.yml`/`data/hero.yml` links.
65	- Everything else on the homepage (`hero`, `about`, `resume`, `testimonials`, etc.) is YAML in
66	  `data/`, not Markdown content.
67	
68	**Static verification files** like `static/.well-known/apple-developer-merchantid-domain-association`
69	are opaque payloads required by third parties (Apple Pay) — don't reformat or "clean up" their
70	contents.
71	
72	## Gotchas
73	
74	**Clear the Hugo cache before testing image changes.** `rm -rf public resources/_gen
75	.hugo_build.lock` before `hugo`/`hugo server` when iterating on `.Fill`/`.Resize` calls — Hugo
76	caches processed image variants keyed by the processing-spec string, and stale cached files can
77	mask whether an edit actually took effect.
78	
79	**Responsive images use width-descriptor srcset + `sizes`, not `1x`/`2x`.** Bootstrap 5's grid
80	makes `.row > *` full-width by default below the column's breakpoint (e.g. `col-lg-4` is 100%
81	wide below `lg`), so images/thumbnails often render *larger* on mobile/tablet than in a desktop
82	multi-column layout — the opposite of the naive assumption. Compute real per-breakpoint CSS widths
83	from the actual column math before picking `sizes` breakpoints for any new responsive image.
84	
85	**`featureImageAnchor` front matter** (e.g. `Left`, `TopLeft`, `Center`) overrides Hugo's default
86	`smart` crop anchor for a post's `featureImage`, applied via
87	`.Params.featureImageAnchor | default "smart"` in both the thumbnail and banner `Fill` calls.
88	
89	**Hugo template scoping inside nested `with`/`range`:** `.` becomes the resource (not the page)
90	inside `{{ with resources.Get ... }}`, and `$` still refers to the template's original entrypoint
91	context (not the loop item) inside `{{ range }}` — capture `{{ $post := . }}` before entering
92	nested scopes if you need page fields inside them.
93	
94	**JSON-LD in `<script>` tags needs `| safeJS`** after `| jsonify` — otherwise Go's `html/template`
95	autoescaper treats the bare text as a JS string literal and double-encodes the whole blob.
96	
97	**Cloudflare Rocket Loader** (zone-level, `coaching.begbie.com` only — not `*.pages.dev` previews)
98	rewrites `<script>` `type` attributes, breaking any script not marked `data-cfasync="false"`. If JS
99	works on a PR preview but not production, check this before assuming a code regression.
100	
101	**`gh pr review --approve` always fails** with "Can not approve your own pull request" for this
102	repo/account — expected, not a bug. Skip straight to `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch`.
103	
104	**`git add path1 path2 ...` aborts entirely if any path doesn't exist** (common after `git mv`/`rm`
105	leaves a path already staged) — no files get staged, not just the missing one. Run `git status`
106	first and pass only currently-existing paths.
107	
108	**SVG `<stop>` elements need `style="stop-color: var(--x)"`, not `stop-color="var(--x)"`.** The
109	bare presentation attribute doesn't resolve CSS custom properties — only the `style` attribute
110	goes through the CSS cascade. Applies to any gradient referencing a design token.
111	
112	**Passing a variable into `style="..."` in a Go template trips the CSS-context auto-escaper** —
113	`style="stop-color: {{ $color }}"` silently renders literal `ZgotmplZ` instead of the value if
114	`$color` isn't a compile-time string literal. Build the string with `printf` and pipe through
115	`| safeCSS`: `style="{{ printf "stop-color: %s;" $color | safeCSS }}"`. Always grep the built
116	`public/` output for `ZgotmplZ` after adding a partial with dynamic inline styles — it fails
117	silently, no build error.
118	
119	**Bootstrap's `$utilities` map is trimmed to only what's currently used** (see
120	`themes/portio/assets/scss/_utilities-trim.scss`) — down from Bootstrap's ~50
121	default utility categories to the 11 this site actually uses (`display`,
122	`justify-content`, `align-items`, `margin-top`/`-end`/`-bottom`/`-x`/`-y`,
123	`padding`, `text-align`, `color`), each restricted to only its in-use values.
124	`$grid-breakpoints`/`$container-max-widths` are similarly trimmed to
125	`xs`/`sm`/`md`/`lg` (`xl`/`xxl` unused). If a template needs a stock Bootstrap
126	utility class not already covered (e.g. `mt-3`, `bg-primary`, `rounded-pill`),
127	it silently resolves to no CSS — not a build error. Add the missing key/value
128	to `_utilities-trim.scss` rather than assuming any Bootstrap utility class
129	will work out of the box. The `forms` and `pagination` Bootstrap SCSS modules
130	are no longer imported either (zero/near-zero usage) — re-add the import in
131	`style.scss` if a future page actually needs form controls or paginated
132	listings.
133	
134	**`position: relative` alone does NOT create a stacking context.** A child with `z-index: -1`
135	needs its positioned ancestor to also declare `z-index` (even `z-index: 0`), or the negative
136	z-index escapes to a page-wide shared layer *below every section's own background* — including
137	sections with an opaque background that then paint over it, making the child invisible with no
138	error. Every section that wraps a decorative blob (`.hero`, `.about_content-thumb`,
139	`.blog-preview`, `.breadCrumb`, `.singleBlog__feature`, `.footer`) sets `position: relative;
140	z-index: 0;` together for this reason — keep them paired in any new section.
141	
142	**External links get `target="_blank"` via a render hook, not `unsafe = true`.**
143	`themes/portio/layouts/_default/_markup/render-link.html` compares the link's host against
144	`site.BaseURL` and adds `target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer"` only for external links.
145	Don't re-enable `markup.goldmark.renderer.unsafe` in `config.toml` — it disables HTML
146	sanitization for every markdown field on the site to solve a problem this hook already covers.
147	
148	**Hugo's `smart` crop anchor can clip a portrait's headroom.** For hero/about-style headshots,
149	`Top`-anchored `.Fill` calls are a safer default than `smart` when the subject's hair/head must
150	stay fully visible — `smart` optimizes for overall visual interest, not for "don't cut off the
151	top of the frame."
152	
153	## Deployment

Both pages build and load cleanly. Summary of the fix:

**Root cause:** the `sizes` attribute on blog thumbnails and the blog banner used stale breakpoints (`1200px`/`1400px`) that don't correspond to the site's actual trimmed grid (`xs/sm/md/lg` only, capped at `lg: 992px`, container maxing at 1280px). That mismatch made the browser think the image would render much wider than it actually does, so it picked the largest (900w) srcset candidate even for a 332px-wide card.

**Fix — recomputed from the real grid math** (container: 1280px max, 40px padding; column gutter: 24px):
- `blogCard.html` (3-up grid ≥768px, stacked <768px):
  `(min-width: 1280px) 376px, (min-width: 768px) calc((100vw - 152px) / 3), calc(100vw - 104px)`
- `blog/single.html` banner (always full-width `col-lg-12`):
  `(min-width: 1280px) 1176px, calc(100vw - 104px)`

Built and spot-checked — no `ZgotmplZ`, correct `sizes` present in the generated HTML for both the homepage blog cards and single-post page.

One thing I *didn't* touch: `hero.html`/`aboutSection.html` have the same stale-breakpoint pattern in their `sizes` strings, and on closer look they actually risk the *opposite* problem in the 768–991px range (undersized, not oversized) — worth a follow-up if you want it, but it wasn't what Lighthouse flagged here so I left it alone.

Create a branch. Then Let's fix the hero/about ones too, and open a PR once they're fixed.

**Responsive images use width-descriptor srcset + `sizes`, not `1x`/`2x`.** Bootstrap 5's grid
makes `.row > *` full-width by default below the column's breakpoint (e.g. `col-lg-4` is 100%
wide below `lg`), so images/thumbnails often render *larger* on mobile/tablet than in a desktop
multi-column layout — the opposite of the naive assumption. Compute real per-breakpoint CSS widths

input.new_string:
**Responsive images use width-descriptor srcset + `sizes`, not `1x`/`2x`.** Bootstrap 5's grid
makes `.row > *` full-width by default below the column's breakpoint (e.g. `col-lg-4` is 100%
wide below `lg`), so images/thumbnails often render *larger* on mobile/tablet than in a desktop
multi-column layout — the opposite of the naive assumption. Compute real per-breakpoint CSS widths
from the actual column math before picking `sizes` breakpoints for any new responsive image.

**The exact `sizes` formula for this theme's grid:** container is capped at `$content-max-width`
(1280px, border-box) with 40px padding each side, and Bootstrap's default 24px gutter. For a
column spanning `k` of 12 grid units, rendered image width = `(C + 24) * k/12 - 24`, where
`C = min(vw, 1280) - 80`. This reduces to exactly `C` (i.e. `calc(100vw - 80px)` below the cap) for
a full-width single column — the row's negative margin cancels the column's own gutter padding at
the container edges, so do **not** additionally subtract 24px for a stacked/single-column case (an
easy double-subtraction mistake). Verify against Lighthouse's mobile "oversized image" audit, which
emulates a 412px-wide viewport: `sizes` should evaluate to `412 - 80 = 332px` for any single-column
image at that width — if Lighthouse reports different "displayed dimensions", the formula is wrong.
Also add a srcset candidate near the smallest expected display width (e.g. 332w) — coarse steps
(500w/750w) make browsers overshoot to the next size up even with a correct `sizes` attribute.

input.project_notes:
begbie-hugo responsive image sizes: derived the exact formula for this theme's grid the hard way (two wrong attempts before landing on the right one). Container is capped at 1280px (border-box), 40px padding each side, Bootstrap default 24px gutter. General column formula: width = (C+24)*k/12 - 24, where C = min(vw,1280)-80 and k = Bootstrap column span out of 12. Key trap: this reduces to exactly C for a full-width single column (stacked/mobile case) — the row's negative margin cancels the column's own gutter padding at the container edges. My first attempt subtracted an extra 24px gutter in that single-column case (used vw-104 instead of vw-80), which was wrong and I only caught it because Lighthouse's numbers didn't match. Great verification trick: Lighthouse's mobile "oversized image" audit emulates a fixed 412px-wide viewport (Moto G Power preset), so `sizes` should evaluate to exactly vw-80=332px for any single-column image — if Lighthouse reports different displayed dimensions than your formula predicts, the formula is wrong, and you can back-derive the correct constant from the reported number.

Second learning: correct `sizes` math alone isn't sufficient — also need a srcset width candidate close to the smallest expected display width. Coarse steps (500w/750w) make the browser overshoot to the next size up even when sizes correctly reports 332px, because required = displayWidth * devicePixelRatio and the gap between available steps is too coarse. Added explicit 332w candidates matching existing crop aspect ratios (332x415 for a 4:5 crop, 332x208 for a 1.6:1 crop) once Lighthouse's reported "displayed dimensions" confirmed the exact aspect ratio was already right.

Documented the full formula and the verification trick directly in the project's CLAUDE.md gotchas section so this doesn't need to be re-derived next time.
input.technical_insights:
Debugging responsive image `sizes` attributes against Lighthouse: Lighthouse's "oversized images" mobile audit uses a fixed emulated viewport (412px, Moto G Power) and reports "displayed dimensions" for the flagged image. Those displayed dimensions are a precise oracle you can back-solve your CSS width formula against — e.g. if you expect `calc(100vw - Xpx)` and Lighthouse says the image actually displayed at 332px at its 412px viewport, then X must be 80, not whatever you assumed. This turns a fuzzy "is my sizes attribute right" question into an exact equation. Much faster than trying to reason about Bootstrap gutter/negative-margin cancellation from first principles (which I got wrong twice) — reason forward to a hypothesis, then use the real tool's reported numbers to falsify/correct it.

Also confirmed: Bootstrap 5's row negative-margin (-gutter/2 each side) cancels the column's own padding (+gutter/2 each side) only at the two outer edges of a row — for a single full-width column that's both edges simultaneously, so the whole gutter cancels and effective width equals the container's content width exactly. For multi-column rows, only the outermost columns get one edge canceled; interior gutters between columns are real and visible. Easy to mentally conflate "gutter exists in the CSS" with "gutter reduces rendered width" — it only does the latter between siblings, not at the container boundary.

My first fix double-subtracted that 24px gutter in the mobile stacked case.

The fix: Lighthouse's mobile audit emulates a fixed 412px viewport and reports exact "displayed dimensions" for flagged images. Used that as an oracle to back-solve the real constant (332px = 412 - 80, not 412 - 104) instead of re-deriving Bootstrap's box model from first principles a third time. 🔍✅

Lesson: when you have a real tool reporting ground-truth numbers, use it to falsify your formula instead of staring harder at the CSS.

# CLAUDE.md Optimizer

## Overview

Analyze, score, and optimize CLAUDE.md files and related configuration (.claude/rules/, MEMORY.md)
to maximize Claude Code's instruction-following quality while minimizing context token usage.

**Critical insight**: Claude Code injects ALL CLAUDE.md content into EVERY API request (~12KB overhead),
and this content accumulates in message history. After 30 turns, overhead exceeds 1MB.
Every byte saved compounds across the entire session.

## Injection Order (how Claude Code loads your config)

Claude Code concatenates config in this fixed order per request:
1. `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (global user instructions)
2. `~/.claude/rules/*.md` (global rules)
3. Project `CLAUDE.md` (root or `.claude/CLAUDE.md`)
4. Project `.claude/rules/*.md` (project rules)
5. `MEMORY.md` (auto-memory)

All wrapped in `<system-reminder>` XML tags. This order affects attention placement strategy:
- Content in position 1 (global) gets strong primacy attention
- Content in position 3-4 (project) is in the middle zone - needs careful prioritization
- Content in position 5 (memory) gets recency attention boost

## Safety Rules

- Never modify files without explicit user approval
- Extract content verbatim when moving to sub-documents (never summarize or paraphrase)
- Verify zero information loss after every extraction
- Back up original files before restructuring
- Skip optimization if CLAUDE.md is already under 80 lines and well-structured

## Workflow

### Step 1: Analyze Current State

Run the analysis script to get a baseline score and identify issues:

```bash
python3 scripts/analyze_claude_md.py <project-dir>
```

Pass `--json` for machine-readable output. If no project directory is specified, the current
working directory is used. The script scans:

- Project-level `CLAUDE.md` (root or `.claude/CLAUDE.md`)
- User-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`
- All `.claude/rules/*.md` files (project and user level)
- `MEMORY.md` files

The report includes:
- Per-file metrics (lines, tokens, structure quality)
- **Session cost estimate** (per-request cost x estimated turns)
- **Non-English content detection** with token overhead estimate
- **Cross-file duplicate detection** (content repeated between files)
- Anti-pattern identification and actionable fixes

Present the analysis report to the user, highlighting the score and top issues.

### Step 2: Review Optimization Rules

Read `references/optimization-rules.md` for the complete set of rules, limits, and best practices.
Use this reference to identify which optimizations apply to the user's specific files.

### Step 3: Apply Optimizations

Apply the following optimizations in priority order, always confirming changes with the user:

**Priority 0 - Language optimization (if applicable)**
- Detect non-English content in CLAUDE.md files
- English instructions use ~30-50% fewer tokens than CJK languages (Korean, Japanese, Chinese)
- Convert non-English instructions to English while preserving technical terms
- Exception: Domain glossary terms, proper nouns, and user-facing strings stay in original language
- This single change can save 2000-4000 tokens per request for CJK-heavy files

**Priority 1 - Remove bloat (highest impact)**
- Delete instructions Claude already follows by default
- Remove content duplicated across files (cross-file dedup)
- Remove content that belongs in linter/formatter configs (.editorconfig, .prettierrc, .eslintrc)
- Remove vague/non-actionable instructions ("follow best practices", "keep code clean")

**Priority 2 - Restructure for efficiency**
- Convert paragraph text to bullet-point lists
- Rewrite instructions in imperative form ("Use X" not "We use X")
- Replace inline code snippets (over 5 lines) with file:line references
- Keep short code patterns (3-5 lines) inline - moving them forces re-derivation
- Move task-specific content to .claude/rules/ with appropriate glob patterns

**Priority 3 - Apply progressive disclosure**
- Categorize content: Essential (every session), Reference (occasional), Redundant (remove)
- Move Reference content to sub-documents via verbatim extraction
- Add a Sub-Documentation Table at the top of CLAUDE.md with links
- Add trigger conditions to each reference ("Read X when modifying Y")
- Keep Essential content inline, never extract it

**Priority 4 - Optimize attention placement (injection-order aware)**
- Place critical prohibitions and key commands at the top of each file
- Place reference trigger index at the bottom
- LLMs have U-shaped attention: strongest at beginning and end, weakest in middle
- Account for injection order: global CLAUDE.md gets primacy, Memory gets recency
- Project CLAUDE.md is mid-injection - front-load its most critical content
- Avoid duplicating global prohibitions in project files (they already have strong attention)

**Priority 5 - Add missing essentials**
- Add project summary one-liner if missing
- Add key directory paths if missing
- Add exact build/test/lint commands with flags if missing
- Add explicit prohibitions ("DO NOT" list) if missing
- Add domain glossary (5-10 terms) if specialized project

**Priority 6 - Modularize**
- Extract concern-specific rules into .claude/rules/ files
- Add YAML glob headers to rule files for targeted loading
- Target 3-5 rule files for optimal modular coverage

**Priority 7 - Future-proof**
- Add an "Information Recording Principles" section to prevent future bloat
- Define what belongs in CLAUDE.md vs rules/ vs docs/ vs code comments
- Establish a pattern for where new instructions should go
- Recommend periodic `/clear` usage to reset accumulated message overhead

### Step 4: Validate

Run the analysis script again on the optimized files to verify improvement:

```bash
python3 scripts/analyze_claude_md.py <project-dir>
```

Verification checklist:
- Score improved from baseline
- Zero information loss (all content either kept inline, moved to sub-doc, or intentionally removed with user approval)
- Essential content still inline
- All sub-document links are valid relative paths
- CI/build scripts that parse CLAUDE.md still work
- Session cost estimate reduced

Present before/after comparison: line counts, token estimates, session cost, and score.

## Target Metrics

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Project CLAUDE.md | under 150 lines |
| User CLAUDE.md | under 50 lines |
| Rule files | under 30 lines each, 3-5 files |
| MEMORY.md | under 200 lines |
| Total all sources | under 250 lines |
| Optimization score | 80+ |
| Information loss | 0% |
| Non-English ratio | under 10% (convert to English) |
| Cross-file duplicates | 0 |

## Key Anti-Patterns to Fix

- Non-English instructions where English would be more token-efficient
- Content duplicated across global and project files (cross-file redundancy)
- Formatting/style rules that belong in linters (eslint, prettier, editorconfig)
- Inline code blocks over 5 lines (replace with file:line references)
- Narrative paragraphs (convert to bullet lists)
- Vague directives ("follow best practices", "keep code clean")
- Instructions for default Claude behavior
- Reference content without trigger conditions
- Critical instructions buried in the middle of the document
- Global prohibitions repeated in project CLAUDE.md (already injected first with strong attention)

## Resources

- `scripts/analyze_claude_md.py` - Analysis and scoring tool (0-100 score, anti-pattern detection, session cost, language detection, cross-file dedup)
- `references/optimization-rules.md` - Complete optimization rules, limits, progressive disclosure patterns, and checklist

1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
4	
5	## What this is
6	
7	A Hugo static site for Rod Begbie's personal/coaching site (`coaching.begbie.com`), using a
8	vendored copy of the **portio** theme (Bootstrap 5.3.8). Deployed to Cloudflare Pages via `wrangler`.
9	
10	## Commands
11	
12	```bash
13	hugo server -D        # local dev server with drafts, live reload
14	hugo                   # build to public/ (generated, gitignored)
15	```
16	
17	Hugo version is pinned to **0.163.3** — set via `HUGO_VERSION` in `wrangler.jsonc`, which is what
18	Cloudflare Pages reads to pick the build image. There is no package.json, Makefile, or test suite;
19	content/template correctness is verified by building and checking `public/` or the dev server.
20	
21	## Architecture
22	
23	**Theme is vendored, not a submodule.** `themes/portio` is a full copy of the theme committed
24	directly into this repo (see commit `55bde54`, "Copy in the portio theme, instead of submoduling
25	it"). `.gitmodules` exists but is empty — don't expect `git submodule update` to do anything.
26	Theme edits happen directly under `themes/portio/layouts`; there is no project-level `layouts/`
27	override directory.
28	
29	**Homepage is built from toggleable, data-driven sections.** Each section partial in
30	`themes/portio/layouts/partials/` (e.g. `aboutSection.html`, `heroSection` via `hero.html`,
31	`resumeSection.html`, `serviceSection.html`, `skillSection.html`, `portfolioSection.html`,
32	`testimonialSection.html`, `blogSection.html`) reads its content from a same-named YAML file in
33	`data/` (e.g. `data/aboutSection.yml`). Every data file starts with `enable: true|false`, and the
34	partial wraps itself in `{{ if .enable }}` — flip that flag to show/hide a section, no template
35	changes needed. Currently disabled: `portfolioSection`, `serviceSection`, `skillSection`.
36	
37	**Data is read via `hugo.Data.<name>`, not `.Site.Data.<name>`.** This was changed in commit
38	`d4d2e42` ("Fix Hugo compatibility for modern versions") when upgrading to Hugo 0.157 — `.Site.Data`
39	is deprecated and `resources.ToCSS` was replaced with `css.Sass`. If you add a new data-driven
40	section or touch SCSS compilation, follow these same patterns rather than older portio-theme
41	examples found online (`themes/portio/exampleSite` still uses the old API and is reference-only,
42	not built).
43	
44	**Design tokens live in `themes/portio/assets/scss/_tokens.scss`.** CSS custom properties
45	(`--accent`, `--ink`, `--bg`, etc.) plus Sass variables for fonts/spacing/radii. New components
46	should reference these, not hardcode hex/px values.
47	
48	**Decorative blob backgrounds use `layouts/partials/blob.html`**, called as
49	`{{ partial "blob.html" (dict "id" "unique-gradient-id" "color" "var(--accent)" "stopOpacity" ".08") }}`
50	inside a `.blob-wrap` wrapper div. One shared partial for every section's blob — don't hand-copy
51	the inline SVG again.
52	
53	**Résumé data is split into `recent`/`earlier` lists**, not one flat list sliced by `first N`.
54	`data/resumeSection.yml`'s `recent` entries render as cards; `earlier` entries generate the
55	"Earlier: ..." summary line directly in the template. Add a new job by moving the oldest `recent`
56	entry into `earlier`, not by editing prose by hand.
57	
58	**Content types:**
59	- `content/blog/*.md` — blog posts (front matter: `title`, `date`, `featureImage`,
60	  `featureImageAnchor` (optional, overrides smart-crop anchor — see below), `subtitle`), rendered
61	  through `themes/portio/layouts/blog/{list,single}.html`.
62	- There is no `/contact/` page — removed (was unlinked from nav/hero/footer, its form posted to an
63	  unconfigured Formspree placeholder). Site contact is the homepage's `#contact` footer CTA
64	  (mailto link) or `data/aboutSection.yml`/`data/hero.yml` links.
65	- Everything else on the homepage (`hero`, `about`, `resume`, `testimonials`, etc.) is YAML in
66	  `data/`, not Markdown content.
67	
68	**Static verification files** like `static/.well-known/apple-developer-merchantid-domain-association`
69	are opaque payloads required by third parties (Apple Pay) — don't reformat or "clean up" their
70	contents.
71	
72	## Gotchas
73	
74	**Clear the Hugo cache before testing image changes.** `rm -rf public resources/_gen
75	.hugo_build.lock` before `hugo`/`hugo server` when iterating on `.Fill`/`.Resize` calls — Hugo
76	caches processed image variants keyed by the processing-spec string, and stale cached files can
77	mask whether an edit actually took effect.
78	
79	**Responsive images use width-descriptor srcset + `sizes`, not `1x`/`2x`.** Bootstrap 5's grid
80	makes `.row > *` full-width by default below the column's breakpoint (e.g. `col-lg-4` is 100%
81	wide below `lg`), so images/thumbnails often render *larger* on mobile/tablet than in a desktop
82	multi-column layout — the opposite of the naive assumption. Compute real per-breakpoint CSS widths
83	from the actual column math before picking `sizes` breakpoints for any new responsive image.
84	
85	**The exact `sizes` formula for this theme's grid:** container is capped at `$content-max-width`
86	(1280px, border-box) with 40px padding each side, and Bootstrap's default 24px gutter. For a
87	column spanning `k` of 12 grid units, rendered image width = `(C + 24) * k/12 - 24`, where
88	`C = min(vw, 1280) - 80`. This reduces to exactly `C` (i.e. `calc(100vw - 80px)` below the cap) for
89	a full-width single column — the row's negative margin cancels the column's own gutter padding at
90	the container edges, so do **not** additionally subtract 24px for a stacked/single-column case (an
91	easy double-subtraction mistake). Verify against Lighthouse's mobile "oversized image" audit, which
92	emulates a 412px-wide viewport: `sizes` should evaluate to `412 - 80 = 332px` for any single-column
93	image at that width — if Lighthouse reports different "displayed dimensions", the formula is wrong.
94	Also add a srcset candidate near the smallest expected display width (e.g. 332w) — coarse steps
95	(500w/750w) make browsers overshoot to the next size up even with a correct `sizes` attribute.
96	
97	**`featureImageAnchor` front matter** (e.g. `Left`, `TopLeft`, `Center`) overrides Hugo's default
98	`smart` crop anchor for a post's `featureImage`, applied via
99	`.Params.featureImageAnchor | default "smart"` in both the thumbnail and banner `Fill` calls.
100	
101	**Hugo template scoping inside nested `with`/`range`:** `.` becomes the resource (not the page)
102	inside `{{ with resources.Get ... }}`, and `$` still refers to the template's original entrypoint
103	context (not the loop item) inside `{{ range }}` — capture `{{ $post := . }}` before entering
104	nested scopes if you need page fields inside them.
105	
106	**JSON-LD in `<script>` tags needs `| safeJS`** after `| jsonify` — otherwise Go's `html/template`
107	autoescaper treats the bare text as a JS string literal and double-encodes the whole blob.
108	
109	**Cloudflare Rocket Loader** (zone-level, `coaching.begbie.com` only — not `*.pages.dev` previews)
110	rewrites `<script>` `type` attributes, breaking any script not marked `data-cfasync="false"`. If JS
111	works on a PR preview but not production, check this before assuming a code regression.
112	
113	**`gh pr review --approve` always fails** with "Can not approve your own pull request" for this
114	repo/account — expected, not a bug. Skip straight to `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch`.
115	
116	**`git add path1 path2 ...` aborts entirely if any path doesn't exist** (common after `git mv`/`rm`
117	leaves a path already staged) — no files get staged, not just the missing one. Run `git status`
118	first and pass only currently-existing paths.
119	
120	**SVG `<stop>` elements need `style="stop-color: var(--x)"`, not `stop-color="var(--x)"`.** The
121	bare presentation attribute doesn't resolve CSS custom properties — only the `style` attribute
122	goes through the CSS cascade. Applies to any gradient referencing a design token.
123	
124	**Passing a variable into `style="..."` in a Go template trips the CSS-context auto-escaper** —
125	`style="stop-color: {{ $color }}"` silently renders literal `ZgotmplZ` instead of the value if
126	`$color` isn't a compile-time string literal. Build the string with `printf` and pipe through
127	`| safeCSS`: `style="{{ printf "stop-color: %s;" $color | safeCSS }}"`. Always grep the built
128	`public/` output for `ZgotmplZ` after adding a partial with dynamic inline styles — it fails
129	silently, no build error.
130	
131	**Bootstrap's `$utilities` map is trimmed to only what's currently used** (see
132	`themes/portio/assets/scss/_utilities-trim.scss`) — down from Bootstrap's ~50
133	default utility categories to the 11 this site actually uses (`display`,

148	z-index escapes to a page-wide shared layer *below every section's own background* — including

1	# CLAUDE.md Optimization Rules Reference
2	
3	## Token Compounding: Why Every Byte Matters
4	
5	Claude Code sends ALL CLAUDE.md content with EVERY API request. The cost compounds:
6	
7	| Scenario | Per-Request Overhead | After 30 Turns (cumulative) |
8	|----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
9	| 100-line CLAUDE.md (~1.5KB) | ~400 tokens | ~12,000 tokens |
10	| 250-line CLAUDE.md (~4KB) | ~1,000 tokens | ~30,000 tokens |
11	| 500-line CLAUDE.md (~8KB) | ~2,000 tokens | ~60,000 tokens |
12	
13	**Session cost formula**: `per_request_tokens x num_turns x 2` (request + history accumulation)
14	
15	Use `/clear` periodically to reset accumulated message history overhead.
16	
17	## Injection Order
18	
19	Claude Code concatenates config in this fixed order:
20	1. `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (global) - gets **primacy attention**
21	2. `~/.claude/rules/*.md` (global rules)
22	3. Project `CLAUDE.md` - **middle zone** (weakest attention)
23	4. Project `.claude/rules/*.md`
24	5. `MEMORY.md` - gets **recency attention**
25	
26	Implications:
27	- Global prohibitions are already well-attended - do not repeat in project files
28	- Front-load project CLAUDE.md with its most critical unique content
29	- Memory content gets natural recency boost
30	
31	## Language Optimization
32	
33	English instructions are significantly more token-efficient:
34	
35	| Language | Avg Tokens per Instruction | vs English |
36	|----------|---------------------------|------------|
37	| English | ~15 tokens | baseline |
38	| Korean | ~22 tokens | +47% |
39	| Japanese | ~25 tokens | +67% |
40	| Chinese | ~20 tokens | +33% |
41	
42	**Rule**: Write CLAUDE.md instructions in English. Keep only these in original language:
43	- Domain-specific glossary terms
44	- Proper nouns and project names
45	- User-facing string patterns that must match exactly
46	
47	A typical 150-line Korean CLAUDE.md saves ~1,000 tokens per request when converted to English.
48	
49	## Line Count Limits
50	
51	| File | Max Lines | Optimal |
52	|------|-----------|---------|
53	| Project CLAUDE.md | 150 | under 100 |
54	| User ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md | 50 | under 30 |
55	| Individual .claude/rules/*.md | 30 | under 20 |
56	| MEMORY.md | 200 | under 100 |
57	| Total across all sources | 250 | under 180 |
58	
59	## Instruction Capacity
60	
61	- Frontier LLMs follow ~150-200 instructions with reasonable consistency
62	- Claude Code system prompt uses ~50 instructions
63	- Remaining capacity: ~100-150 instructions for CLAUDE.md + rules
64	- Instruction-following quality degrades linearly as count increases
65	- Instructions at prompt peripheries get more attention than middle ones (U-shaped attention)
66	
67	## Content Tier Classification
68	
69	Classify every section before optimizing:
70	
71	| Tier | Criteria | Action |
72	|------|----------|--------|
73	| Essential | Used every session, unlocks core workflow, build/test commands, prohibitions | Keep inline in CLAUDE.md |
74	| Reference | Occasional use, detailed specs, edge cases, historical decisions | Extract to sub-document with trigger condition |
75	| Redundant | Duplicates existing docs, restates Claude defaults, belongs in linter config | Remove (with user approval) |
76	
77	### Classification Questions
78	1. Is this consulted in more than 50% of sessions? -> Essential
79	2. Does violating this cause severe consequences? -> Essential
80	3. Is this a copy-paste code pattern under 5 lines? -> Essential (keep inline)
81	4. Can this be triggered by a specific file/task context? -> Reference
82	5. Is this detailed SOP, edge case handling, or historical record? -> Reference
83	
84	## Progressive Disclosure Patterns
85	
86	### Sub-Documentation Table
87	Place at the top of CLAUDE.md to link extracted content:
88	```
89	| Topic | File | When to read |
90	|-------|------|-------------|
91	| API specs | docs/api-spec.md | When modifying API endpoints |
92	| DB schema | docs/schema.md | When writing database queries |
93	```
94	
95	### Trigger Conditions
96	Every reference document needs a trigger condition stating when to load it.
97	Without triggers, extracted content becomes invisible and never gets used.
98	
99	BAD: "See docs/api.md for details"
100	GOOD: "Read docs/api.md when modifying files in src/api/ or adding new endpoints"
101	
102	### Attention-Optimized Placement
103	LLMs exhibit U-shaped attention (primacy + recency effect):
104	- TOP of file: Prohibitions, critical commands, project summary
105	- MIDDLE of file: Informational content, directory structure, glossary
106	- BOTTOM of file: Reference trigger index, sub-documentation table (second copy)
107	
108	## Must-Include Sections
109	
110	1. **Project summary** - One-liner orientation (tech stack, purpose)
111	2. **Directory structure** - Key paths and what they contain
112	3. **Commands** - Exact build/test/lint/deploy commands with flags
113	4. **Prohibitions** - Explicit "DO NOT" statements (more effective than positive recommendations)
114	5. **Domain glossary** - 5-10 key terms (if specialized domain)
115	6. **Information recording principles** - Rules for where new instructions go (prevents future bloat)
116	
117	## Must-Avoid Anti-Patterns
118	
119	### Non-English Instructions (for non-English users)
120	- CJK characters use 2-3x more tokens than equivalent English
121	- CLAUDE.md is injected into EVERY request - the overhead compounds
122	- Convert instructions to English; keep only domain terms in original language
123	- "PascalCase for public methods" is 5 tokens; the Korean equivalent is 8+ tokens
124	
125	### Cross-File Duplicates
126	- Global CLAUDE.md and project CLAUDE.md are concatenated - duplicates waste tokens twice
127	- Common duplicates: coding style rules, git conventions, testing guidelines
128	- Rule: If it's in global, do NOT repeat in project. Global gets primacy attention.
129	- Check for semantic duplicates too (same instruction, different wording)
130	
131	### Code Style in CLAUDE.md
132	- Never send an LLM to do a linter's job
133	- Formatting rules belong in .editorconfig, .prettierrc, .eslintrc
134	- LLMs are 100x more expensive than linters for this task
135	- Move to hooks: pre-commit formatters, post-write linters
136	
137	### Inline Code Snippets
138	- Long code snippets become outdated quickly
139	- Use `file:line` references to point to authoritative source
140	- Exception: 3-5 line patterns that demonstrate a convention (keep inline)
141	- Moving short code patterns to references forces LLM to re-derive or make extra reads
142	
143	### Vague Instructions
144	- BAD: "Format code properly", "Follow best practices", "Keep code clean"
145	- GOOD: "Use PascalCase for public methods", "Run `npm test` before committing"
146	
147	### Narrative Paragraphs
148	- Claude processes bullet points more efficiently than paragraphs
149	- Convert multi-sentence paragraphs to concise list items
150	- Each instruction should be one clear directive
151	
152	### Redundant Content
153	- Remove instructions for things Claude already does correctly
154	- Remove content duplicated in official documentation
155	- Remove instructions that contradict each other
156	
157	### References Without Triggers
158	- Extracted content without trigger conditions is effectively deleted
159	- Every sub-document link needs "Read X when Y"
160	- Content that is never triggered wastes the extraction effort
161	
162	## Structure Best Practices
163	
164	### Use Imperative Form
165	- GOOD: "Use functional components"
166	- BAD: "The project uses functional components"
167	- GOOD: "Run tests with `npm test --coverage`"
168	- BAD: "Tests can be run using npm test"
169	
170	### Modular Rules (.claude/rules/)
171	- Create separate .md files per concern (testing, security, API, frontend)
172	- Use glob patterns in YAML headers for auto-loading
173	- Performance: 40% context noise reduction, 35% relevance improvement
174	- Recommended: 3-5 rule files minimum
175	
176	### Information Recording Principles
177	Add a section defining where new instructions belong to prevent future bloat:
178	- Always-needed rules -> CLAUDE.md
179	- File-type-specific rules -> .claude/rules/ with glob patterns
180	- Detailed procedures -> docs/ referenced by trigger conditions
181	- Temporary notes -> MEMORY.md (with expiry intent)
182	
183	## Safety Rules for Extraction
184	
185	- Always back up before modifying
186	- Extract content verbatim (never summarize or paraphrase during migration)
187	- Verify zero information loss via diff after extraction
188	- Keep encryption/auth unlock instructions inline (chicken-and-egg problem)
189	- Preserve content that CI scripts parse via regex
190	- Require explicit user approval before any file modification
191	
192	## Performance Metrics
193	
194	| Optimization | Impact |
195	|-------------|--------|
196	| Well-structured CLAUDE.md | 35% fewer corrections |
197	| Modular rules | 25% less setup time between sessions |
198	| Token optimization | 60% context savings (4500 to 1800 tokens) |
199	| Declared critical paths | 50% less file search time |
200	| Code examples (5-line) | 40% fewer corrections vs 20-line descriptions |
201	| Validation commands | 30% less back-and-forth |
202	| Repository-specific tuning | +10.87% accuracy on SWE Bench |
203	| Progressive disclosure | 62% line reduction with 0% info loss (typical) |
204	
205	## Session Management
206	
207	- Use `/clear` after 20-30 turns to reset accumulated message history
208	- Skills persist in context until `/clear` - avoid invoking unnecessary skills
209	- MCP tools are lazy-loaded (deferred) - unused ones cost minimal tokens
210	- Sub-agents do NOT inherit conversation history - they start fresh
211	
212	## Checklist for Optimized CLAUDE.md
213	
214	- [ ] Instructions written in English (non-English converted)
215	- [ ] No cross-file duplicates (global vs project vs rules)
216	- [ ] Under 150 lines (project) / 50 lines (user)
217	- [ ] All instructions in imperative form
218	- [ ] No formatting/style rules (use linter configs instead)
219	- [ ] No inline code snippets over 5 lines (use file:line refs)
220	- [ ] Short code patterns (3-5 lines) kept inline
221	- [ ] No vague instructions
222	- [ ] No duplicate content within files
223	- [ ] Key commands documented with exact flags
224	- [ ] Critical paths declared explicitly
225	- [ ] Prohibition list at top of file
226	- [ ] 3+ modular rule files in .claude/rules/
227	- [ ] Domain glossary if specialized project
228	- [ ] MEMORY.md under 200 lines, organized by topic
229	- [ ] Sub-documentation table with trigger conditions
230	- [ ] Information recording principles section included
231	- [ ] Critical content at top/bottom (U-shaped attention aware of injection order)
232	- [ ] Zero information loss verified after extraction
233	- [ ] Session cost estimate under 1,000 tokens per request

## Factual Finding

Claude Code **does support path-scoped rule loading in `.claude/rules/`**, but NOT in the way the third-party skill claims.

### What's Actually Supported

From the official documentation in `https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory.md`:

Rules in `.claude/rules/` can use YAML frontmatter with a `paths` field to scope instructions to specific file patterns:

```markdown
---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
  - "lib/**/*.ts"
---

# API Development Rules
- All endpoints must include input validation
```

**Key behavior:**
- Rules WITH `paths` frontmatter load **when Claude reads files matching those patterns**, not on every request
- Rules WITHOUT `paths` frontmatter load **unconditionally at launch** (same as `CLAUDE.md`)
- This applies only to `.claude/rules/*.md` files, not user/project-level CLAUDE.md

### What's NOT Supported

The skill's claims do not match official behavior:

- ❌ No `globs: "**/*.scss"` frontmatter syntax (uses `paths` array instead)
- ❌ No "40% context noise reduction" claim found in any official documentation
- ❌ No mention of "35% relevance improvement"
- ❌ Rules are not "auto-loaded conditionally" — they're loaded when you edit matching files, but still enter full context at that point

### Real Benefit vs. Overselling

The actual feature (path-scoped rules) *does* reduce context bloat compared to a single monolithic CLAUDE.md, but it's a modest, file-type-aware organization tool—not an automatic context optimization feature with those specific improvement percentages.

**Recommendation:** You can safely use `.claude/rules/` with `paths` frontmatter to organize project instructions by domain (API rules, frontend rules, testing rules), and Claude Code will load them appropriately. But don't expect it to automatically reduce context by 40% or improve relevance by 35%. Those numbers appear to be unsupported claims from the skill.</result>

# Hugo Template Gotchas

- **Clear the Hugo cache before testing image changes.** Run `rm -rf public resources/_gen
  .hugo_build.lock` before `hugo`/`hugo server` when iterating on `.Fill`/`.Resize` calls — Hugo
  caches processed image variants keyed by the processing-spec string, and stale cached files can
  mask whether an edit actually took effect.

- **Responsive images use width-descriptor srcset + `sizes`, not `1x`/`2x`.** Bootstrap 5's grid
  makes `.row > *` full-width by default below the column's breakpoint (e.g. `col-lg-4` is 100%
  wide below `lg`), so images/thumbnails often render *larger* on mobile/tablet than in a desktop
  multi-column layout — the opposite of the naive assumption. Compute real per-breakpoint CSS
  widths from the actual column math before picking `sizes` breakpoints for any new responsive
  image.

- **The exact `sizes` formula for this theme's grid:** container is capped at
  `$content-max-width` (1280px, border-box) with 40px padding each side, and Bootstrap's default
  24px gutter. For a column spanning `k` of 12 grid units, rendered image width =
  `(C + 24) * k/12 - 24`, where `C = min(vw, 1280) - 80`. This reduces to exactly `C` (i.e.
  `calc(100vw - 80px)` below the cap) for a full-width single column — the row's negative margin
  cancels the column's own gutter padding at the container edges, so do **not** additionally
  subtract 24px for a stacked/single-column case (an easy double-subtraction mistake). Verify
  against Lighthouse's mobile "oversized image" audit, which emulates a 412px-wide viewport:
  `sizes` should evaluate to `412 - 80 = 332px` for any single-column image at that width — if
  Lighthouse reports different "displayed dimensions", the formula is wrong. Also add a srcset
  candidate near the smallest expected display width (e.g. 332w) — coarse steps (500w/750w) make
  browsers overshoot to the next size up even with a correct `sizes` attribute.

- **`featureImageAnchor` front matter** (e.g. `Left`, `TopLeft`, `Center`) overrides Hugo's
  default `smart` crop anchor for a post's `featureImage`, applied via
  `.Params.featureImageAnchor | default "smart"` in both the thumbnail and banner `Fill` calls.

- **Hugo's `smart` crop anchor can clip a portrait's headroom.** For hero/about-style headshots,
  `Top`-anchored `.Fill` calls are a safer default than `smart` when the subject's hair/head must
  stay fully visible — `smart` optimizes for overall visual interest, not for "don't cut off the
  top of the frame."

- **Hugo template scoping inside nested `with`/`range`:** `.` becomes the resource (not the page)
  inside `{{ with resources.Get ... }}`, and `$` still refers to the template's original
  entrypoint context (not the loop item) inside `{{ range }}` — capture `{{ $post := . }}` before
  entering nested scopes if you need page fields inside them.

- **JSON-LD in `<script>` tags needs `| safeJS`** after `| jsonify` — otherwise Go's
  `html/template` autoescaper treats the bare text as a JS string literal and double-encodes the
  whole blob.

- **Passing a variable into `style="..."` in a Go template trips the CSS-context auto-escaper** —
  `style="stop-color: {{ $color }}"` silently renders literal `ZgotmplZ` instead of the value if
  `$color` isn't a compile-time string literal. Build the string with `printf` and pipe through
  `| safeCSS`: `style="{{ printf "stop-color: %s;" $color | safeCSS }}"`. Always grep the built
  `public/` output for `ZgotmplZ` after adding a partial with dynamic inline styles — it fails
  silently, no build error.

- **External links get `target="_blank"` via a render hook, not `unsafe = true`.**
  `themes/portio/layouts/_default/_markup/render-link.html` compares the link's host against
  `site.BaseURL` and adds `target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer"` only for external links.
  Don't re-enable `markup.goldmark.renderer.unsafe` in `config.toml` — it disables HTML
  sanitization for every markdown field on the site to solve a problem this hook already covers.

# SCSS/CSS Gotchas

- **SVG `<stop>` elements need `style="stop-color: var(--x)"`, not `stop-color="var(--x)"`.** The

- **Bootstrap's `$utilities` map is trimmed to only what's currently used** (see

  categories to the 11 this site actually uses (`display`, `justify-content`, `align-items`,
  `margin-top`/`-end`/`-bottom`/`-x`/`-y`, `padding`, `text-align`, `color`), each restricted to
  only its in-use values. `$grid-breakpoints`/`$container-max-widths` are similarly trimmed to
  `xs`/`sm`/`md`/`lg` (`xl`/`xxl` unused). If a template needs a stock Bootstrap utility class not
  already covered (e.g. `mt-3`, `bg-primary`, `rounded-pill`), it silently resolves to no CSS —
  not a build error. Add the missing key/value to `_utilities-trim.scss` rather than assuming any
  Bootstrap utility class will work out of the box. The `forms` and `pagination` Bootstrap SCSS
  modules are no longer imported either (zero/near-zero usage) — re-add the import in
  `style.scss` if a future page actually needs form controls or paginated listings.

- **`position: relative` alone does NOT create a stacking context.** A child with `z-index: -1`
  needs its positioned ancestor to also declare `z-index` (even `z-index: 0`), or the negative
  z-index escapes to a page-wide shared layer *below every section's own background* — including
  sections with an opaque background that then paint over it, making the child invisible with no
  error. Every section that wraps a decorative blob (`.hero`, `.about_content-thumb`,
  `.blog-preview`, `.breadCrumb`, `.singleBlog__feature`, `.footer`) sets `position: relative;
  z-index: 0;` together for this reason — keep them paired in any new section.

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## What this is

A Hugo static site for Rod Begbie's personal/coaching site (`coaching.begbie.com`), using a
vendored copy of the **portio** theme (Bootstrap 5.3.8). Deployed to Cloudflare Pages via `wrangler`.

## Reference documents

Claude Code auto-loads these when you touch a matching file path — no need to read them manually.

| Topic | File | Loads when touching |
|-------|------|----------------------|
| Hugo templating, image pipeline, responsive `sizes`/srcset | `.claude/rules/hugo-templates.md` | `themes/portio/layouts/**/*.html` |
| Bootstrap utilities trim, SVG/gradient CSS, stacking contexts | `.claude/rules/scss-css.md` | `themes/portio/assets/scss/**/*.scss` |

## Commands

```bash
hugo server -D        # local dev server with drafts, live reload
hugo                   # build to public/ (generated, gitignored)
```

- Hugo version is pinned to **0.163.3** via `HUGO_VERSION` in `wrangler.jsonc` — that's what
  Cloudflare Pages reads to pick the build image.
- No package.json, Makefile, or test suite. Verify content/template correctness by building and
  checking `public/` or the dev server.

## Architecture

- **Theme is vendored, not a submodule.** `themes/portio` is a full copy of the theme committed
  directly into this repo (commit `55bde54`). `.gitmodules` exists but is empty — `git submodule
  update` does nothing. Edit templates directly under `themes/portio/layouts`; there is no
  project-level `layouts/` override directory.
- **Homepage sections are toggleable and data-driven.** Each partial in
  `themes/portio/layouts/partials/` (`aboutSection.html`, `hero.html`, `resumeSection.html`,
  `serviceSection.html`, `skillSection.html`, `portfolioSection.html`, `testimonialSection.html`,
  `blogSection.html`) reads a same-named YAML file in `data/`. Every data file starts with
  `enable: true|false`; the partial wraps itself in `{{ if .enable }}` — flip that flag to
  show/hide a section. Currently disabled: `portfolioSection`, `serviceSection`, `skillSection`.
- **Read site data via `hugo.Data.<name>`, not `.Site.Data.<name>`** (changed in commit `d4d2e42`
  for Hugo 0.157 — `.Site.Data` is deprecated, `resources.ToCSS` became `css.Sass`). Follow this
  pattern for new data-driven sections; `themes/portio/exampleSite` is reference-only (uses the
  old API, not built).
- **Design tokens live in `themes/portio/assets/scss/_tokens.scss`** — CSS custom properties
  (`--accent`, `--ink`, `--bg`) plus Sass variables for fonts/spacing/radii. Reference these in
  new components; don't hardcode hex/px values.
- **Decorative blob backgrounds use `layouts/partials/blob.html`**, called as
  `{{ partial "blob.html" (dict "id" "unique-gradient-id" "color" "var(--accent)" "stopOpacity"
  ".08") }}` inside a `.blob-wrap` wrapper div. Don't hand-copy the inline SVG again.
- **Résumé data is split into `recent`/`earlier` lists**, not one flat list sliced by `first N`.
  `data/resumeSection.yml`'s `recent` entries render as cards; `earlier` entries generate the
  "Earlier: ..." summary line in the template. Add a new job by moving the oldest `recent` entry
  into `earlier`.
- **Content types:** `content/blog/*.md` are blog posts (front matter: `title`, `date`,
  `featureImage`, `featureImageAnchor` (optional), `subtitle`), rendered through
  `themes/portio/layouts/blog/{list,single}.html`. There is no `/contact/` page — removed (was
  unlinked, posted to an unconfigured Formspree placeholder); site contact is the homepage's
  `#contact` footer CTA or `data/aboutSection.yml`/`data/hero.yml` links. Everything else on the
  homepage is YAML in `data/`, not Markdown content.
- **Don't reformat static verification files** like
  `static/.well-known/apple-developer-merchantid-domain-association` — opaque payloads required
  by third parties (Apple Pay).

## Workflow gotchas

- **`gh pr review --approve` always fails** ("Can not approve your own pull request") for this
  repo/account — expected. Skip straight to `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch`.
- **`git add path1 path2 ...` aborts entirely if any path doesn't exist** (common after
  `git mv`/`rm` leaves a path already staged) — no files get staged, not just the missing one.
  Run `git status` first and pass only currently-existing paths.

## Deployment

Cloudflare Pages, configured by `wrangler.jsonc`. `pages_build_output_dir` points at `public/`.
Pages project name is `begbie-hugo` (matches `name` in `wrangler.jsonc`). To check deployment
status/logs, use the `cloudflare-api` MCP tool against
`/accounts/{accountId}/pages/projects/begbie-hugo/deployments` rather than the dashboard.

**Cloudflare Rocket Loader** (zone-level, `coaching.begbie.com` only — not `*.pages.dev`
previews) rewrites `<script>` `type` attributes, breaking any script not marked
`data-cfasync="false"`. If JS works on a PR preview but not production, check this before
assuming a code regression.

## Where new instructions belong

- Always-needed project facts (architecture, commands, workflow) → this file.
- Gotchas scoped to one file type → `.claude/rules/*.md` with a `paths:` frontmatter key, not
  inline here.
- One-off session details → don't record; they belong in the PR/commit, not CLAUDE.md.
